sentences.chinese: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。
sentences.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu and more than ten others in cinnabar garments shaved themselves and applied the collar, following the king to Chang'an.
sentences.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others shaved their heads, donned the convict's collar and russet robes, and followed their king to Chang'an.

sentences.chinese: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。
sentences.literal: The King of Zhao Ao's affair was cleared; he was able to come out; the king was abolished and made Marquis of Xuanping; thereupon he advanced speech about Shu and the ten men.
sentences.idiomatic: When King Zhang Ao's case was cleared, he was released, stripped of his kingship, and enfeoffed as Marquis of Xuanping; he then recommended Tian Shu and the other nine men to the throne.

sentences.chinese: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。
sentences.literal: The Sovereign summoned them for audience and conversed with them; no Han court minister could go out to their right.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor received them in audience and spoke with them; not one minister at the Han court proved their equal.

sentences.chinese: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。
sentences.literal: The Sovereign was pleased; all were appointed commandery governors and marquisate chancellors.
sentences.idiomatic: Pleased, the emperor appointed them all as governors of commanderies or chancellors to feudal princes.

sentences.chinese: 叔為漢中守十餘年。
sentences.literal: Shu was Hanzhong governor for more than ten years.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu served as governor of Hanzhong for more than a decade.

sentences.chinese: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」
sentences.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen first was established, he summoned Shu and asked, saying, "Sir, do you know the elders of the world?"
sentences.idiomatic: Soon after Emperor Wen took the throne, he summoned Tian Shu and asked, "Do you know who the most worthy and steadfast men in the realm are?"

sentences.chinese: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」
sentences.literal: He replied, saying, "How could your servant be sufficient to know them!"
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu replied, "How could a man like me presume to judge such things?"

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」
sentences.literal: The Sovereign said, "Sir is an elder; you ought to know them."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "You are yourself a man of integrity; you should know."

sentences.chinese: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」
sentences.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, "The former Yunzhong governor Meng Shu is an elder."
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "Meng Shu, the former governor of Yunzhong, is such a man."

sentences.chinese: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。
sentences.literal: At this time Meng Shu sat for the raiders greatly entering Yunzhong and was dismissed.
sentences.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been dismissed because the barbarians had overrun Yunzhong on his watch.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。
sentences.literal: The Sovereign said, "The late emperor placed Meng Shu at Yunzhong for more than ten years already; the raiders often entered once; Meng Shu could not firmly defend; for no reason soldiers who died in battle were several hundred.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for over ten years. Whenever the raiders broke in, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of soldiers died in battle for no good reason.

sentences.chinese: 長者固殺人乎？」
sentences.literal: Do elders by nature kill people?"
sentences.idiomatic: Is that how a 'worthy man' behaves—sending men to their deaths?"

sentences.chinese: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！
sentences.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, "As for Guan Gao and others plotting rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would crime three clans; yet Meng Shu shaved himself and applied the collar, followed King Zhang, with body dying for him—how could he have known he would become Yunzhong governor!
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "When Guan Gao and the others plotted treason, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict: in Zhao, anyone who followed King Zhang would suffer kin extermination to the third degree. Yet Meng Shu shaved his head, put on the convict's collar, and followed King Zhang, ready to die at his side. How could he have dreamed he would one day govern Yunzhong!

sentences.chinese: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！
sentences.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; soldiers were exhausted; and the Xiongnu Modu newly had subdued the northern Yi, coming to be border raiders; Meng Shu knew the soldiers were exhausted, could not bear to speak out; gentlemen strove to go to the walls and die against the enemy, like sons for fathers; by this reason those who died were several hundred—how could Meng Shu have driven them!
sentences.idiomatic: Han and Chu were locked in stalemate; the troops were worn out, while Modu of the Xiongnu had just subdued the northern tribes and was raiding the frontier. Meng Shu saw how spent his men were and could not bring himself to order them out; they vied to man the walls and die like sons for a father, which is why hundreds fell—Meng Shu did not drive them to it!

sentences.chinese: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」
sentences.literal: This is the reason Meng Shu is an elder."
sentences.idiomatic: That is precisely why Meng Shu is a true man of worth."

sentences.chinese: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」
sentences.literal: Thereupon the Sovereign said, "Worthy indeed is Meng Shu!"
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "Meng Shu is indeed a worthy man!"

sentences.chinese: 復召以為雲中守。
sentences.literal: Again he summoned him and made him Yunzhong governor.
sentences.idiomatic: He summoned Meng Shu back and reappointed him governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.chinese: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。
sentences.literal: After several years, Shu by sitting in law lost office.
sentences.idiomatic: Some years later Tian Shu lost his post for a legal infraction.

sentences.chinese: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。
sentences.literal: King Xiao of Liang sent a man who killed Han deliberation minister Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang; he fully obtained the affair.
sentences.idiomatic: King Xiao of Liang had had the Han councilor Yuan Ang murdered; Emperor Jing summoned Tian Shu to investigate the affair in Liang, and he uncovered the full truth.

sentences.chinese: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」
sentences.literal: Returning he reported; the Sovereign said, "Did Liang have it?"
sentences.idiomatic: On his return he reported. The emperor asked, "Was Liang involved?"

sentences.chinese: 對曰：「有之。」
sentences.literal: He replied, "There was it."
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, "Yes."

sentences.chinese: 「事安在？」
sentences.literal: "Where is the affair?"
sentences.idiomatic: "Where is the proof?"

sentences.chinese: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。
sentences.literal: Shu said, "The Sovereign has nothing by which to take the Liang affair as question.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, "Your Majesty should not press the matter of Liang.

sentences.chinese: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；
sentences.literal: Now if the King of Liang does not submit to execution, this is abandoning Han law;
sentences.idiomatic: If the king of Liang is not punished, the law of the Han is set aside;

sentences.chinese: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」
sentences.literal: if he submits to execution, the empress dowager will not find her food sweet, will not find her mat secure in lying—this worry lies on Your Majesty."
sentences.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will lose her appetite and her sleep—the burden of that grief will fall on Your Majesty."

sentences.chinese: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the Sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him Lu chancellor.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor admired his judgment and appointed him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.chinese: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。
sentences.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at office, commoners who by the king had taken their goods and property and self-spoke were more than a hundred.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon after the new chancellor took office, more than a hundred commoners came forward claiming the king had seized their property.

sentences.chinese: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？
sentences.literal: Shu took their channel-leaders two thousand men and flogged them, angrily saying to them, "Is not the king your lord?
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu had their ringleaders—some two thousand men—flogged and rebuked them: "Is the king not your sovereign?

sentences.chinese: 何敢自言主！」
sentences.literal: How dare you self-speak against the lord!"
sentences.idiomatic: How dare you denounce your lord to the authorities!"

sentences.chinese: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。
sentences.literal: The King of Lu heard it, was greatly ashamed, issued middle treasury money, had the chancellor repay them.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Lu was deeply shamed; he drew funds from the inner treasury and told the chancellor to make restitution.

sentences.chinese: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」
sentences.literal: The chancellor said, "Let the king himself send men to repay them; if not so, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good."
sentences.idiomatic: The chancellor replied, "Let Your Majesty's own agents pay them back; otherwise it will look as though the king did wrong and the chancellor played the hero."

sentences.chinese: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。
sentences.literal: The King of Lu liked hunting; the chancellor always followed entering the park; the king always rested the chancellor to go to the lodge.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; the chancellor often accompanied him into the hunting park, and the king would dismiss him to the guest quarters to rest.

sentences.chinese: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」
sentences.literal: The chancellor always exposed-sat outside the park, to the end not resting, saying, "My king is exposed to the elements—alone why do I go to lodging?"
sentences.idiomatic: The chancellor would sit in the open outside the park and refuse to leave, saying, "While my king endures wind and weather, why should I take shelter?"

sentences.chinese: 王以故不大出遊。
sentences.literal: The king by this reason did not greatly go out roaming.
sentences.idiomatic: After that the king rarely went out on long hunting expeditions.

sentences.chinese: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」
sentences.literal: Several years he died in office; Lu with a hundred in gold sacrificed; the youngest son Ren did not accept, saying, "By righteousness I do not harm the former man's name."
sentences.idiomatic: Some years later he died in office. The state of Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for his funeral rites, but his youngest son, Ren, refused it, saying, "Duty forbids me to tarnish my father's name."

sentences.chinese: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: Ren by stalwart bravery became the General of Guards' retainer-guest; several times he followed striking the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Ren, bold and brave, became a retainer of the General of the Guards and repeatedly accompanied him on campaigns against the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。
sentences.literal: The General of Guards advanced words making Ren gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand piculs, chancellor chief clerk, and lost office.
sentences.idiomatic: The general recommended him for appointment as a gentleman-attendant; he rose to the rank of two thousand piculs as chief clerk to the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.chinese: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。
sentences.literal: Later he was sent to stab-inspect the Three He; returning, his memorial affairs matched the Sovereign's mind; he was appointed capital-region commandant.
sentences.idiomatic: Later he was dispatched to inspect the Three He region; on his return his report pleased the throne, and he was appointed commandant of the capital area.

sentences.chinese: 月餘，遷司直。
sentences.literal: After more than a month, he was transferred to Director Upright.
sentences.idiomatic: A little over a month later he was promoted to director of upright conduct.

sentences.chinese: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。
sentences.literal: Several years, the Rebellious Heir raised troops; Ren's division closed the city gates, causing the heir to be able to flee; he sat for releasing a rebel and his clan was exterminated.
sentences.idiomatic: Some years later, when the heir apparent Liu Ju mutinied, Tian Ren, whose detachment controlled the city gates, managed their closure in such a way that the heir broke out of the capital; he was convicted of letting a rebel escape and his entire clan was executed.

sentences.chinese: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。
sentences.literal: The appraisal says: By Xiang Yu's spirit, yet Ji Bu by bravery displayed name in Chu; personally treading the army and plucking banners several times—he may be called a stalwart knight.
sentences.idiomatic: The appraisal states: In an age dominated by Xiang Yu's dash, Ji Bu won fame in Chu for his courage; he repeatedly took the field and tore enemy banners from their staffs—a true stalwart.

sentences.chinese: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？
sentences.literal: Reaching to distress and straits, slave shame, living hastily yet not changing—why?
sentences.idiomatic: Yet when he fell to the depths, shamed like a slave, he clung to life without yielding his principles—why was that?

sentences.chinese: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。
sentences.literal: He himself bore his talents; receiving humiliation he was not ashamed; wishing to have something by which to use what was not yet sufficient; therefore in the end he became a Han famed general.
sentences.idiomatic: He trusted in his own abilities; he could endure disgrace without losing heart because he still had more to give the world, and so he ended his career as a celebrated general of the Han.

sentences.chinese: 賢者誠重其死。
sentences.literal: The worthy truly weigh their death.
sentences.idiomatic: A worthy man truly weighs the worth of his life.

sentences.chinese: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。
sentences.literal: As for maids and concubines, base persons, stirred by indignation and killing themselves—not able to be brave; their plan is without sense to the utmost only.
sentences.idiomatic: Maids and concubines, the meanest sort, may work themselves into a passion and take their own lives; that is not courage but the shallowest, most witless impulse.

sentences.chinese: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！
sentences.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao; going to death like returning—they truly knew where they stood; though ancient ardent knights, what could add to them!
sentences.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue at the risk of his life; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao to the edge of death as calmly as if going home. They knew exactly where they stood; the boldest heroes of antiquity could not surpass them.
